reply.original:
Ask yourself two questions.

1) How am I going to generate income to finance my "New Life"?

2) Why would I not just use this method to enjoy my 'current life'?

Reflect.

items.original:

You are both Missing my point and Making my point.

The point is that for 95% of us, this information is useless as we do not and will not have the means to act on it even is we wished to.

If we did have the means to act on this why would we bother as we would have enough of a financial base that unless we were wanted by the Mob, we would not need to hide?

reply.shortExcerpt: "...you want to obfuscate your identity in a way that it's so difficult for people to follow you that anything short of a government task force will lack for the patience or funding to keep doggedly trying to find you..."
reply.original:
"...you want to obfuscate your identity in a way that it's so difficult for people to follow you that anything short of a government task force will lack for the patience or funding to keep doggedly trying to find you..."

And therein lies the problem. The one entity I am most interested in losing contact with is the one entity that has the resources to find you.

There is just no way to completely disappear from the government and do so legally.

Oh well. Interesting read none the less.

items.shortExcerpt: @Zundfolge: Actually, unless you've been very successful at something the gummint is interested in, you're probably of no interest to them as it is. They are actually very easy to "lose" as they really don't care about any of us, so long as we keep a low profile among the "great unwashed."
items.original:
@Zundfolge: Actually, unless you've been very successful at something the gummint is interested in, you're probably of no interest to them as it is. They are actually very easy to "lose" as they really don't care about any of us, so long as we keep a low profile among the "great unwashed."

There are so many fake, erroneous and stolen/converted SSN's in use by illegal aliens and others now that it will be a good century before more than the handful who somehow stick their heads up by significant criminality or notable success (that might trigger tax authorities' or other regulators' interest) are even considered for investigation.

reply.shortExcerpt: You can disappear and still communicate. it's hard to do but you need to cultivate a online persona that is nothing like you. Problem is that any Link to your past is a flashing "LOOK HERE" beacon.
reply.original:
You can disappear and still communicate. it's hard to do but you need to cultivate a online persona that is nothing like you. Problem is that any Link to your past is a flashing "LOOK HERE" beacon.

Used prepaid cellphones are great, they are usually registered to someone you do not know, buy a card and keep it running, in fact get several and be ready to ditch a phone if you think it's getting connected to you.

Internet is easy, Open AP's are abundant. for the first few years use only open ap's and route all your traffic through a onion router.

Finally you can set up a mail service to receive mail and not have it attached to you. Several companies will receive mail for you, open it, scan it, and then email it. earthclassmail.com for example.

One trick I saw a friend use was to find an abandoned home near where he was living and have mail sent there if he needed a parcel. today there are so many abandoned homes that it's easy to do this and never use the same house twice.

Basically you have to live the rest of your life like a spy or a person on the run. The one friend that I had contact with that did do this for 5 years gave up in the end because of the stress. good luck and enjoy the ulcers.

items.shortExcerpt: @timgray: I could probably disappear physically, and still maintain my current online presence. Sure, my online presence is linked to my real identity, but as long as I don't update that information to say that I moved to Canada, it shouldn't make much of a difference.
items.original:
@timgray: I could probably disappear physically, and still maintain my current online presence. Sure, my online presence is linked to my real identity, but as long as I don't update that information to say that I moved to Canada, it shouldn't make much of a difference.

Of course, I'd have to use proxies and the like, just in case the cyberpolice try to backtrace me.
items.display:
@timgray: I could probably disappear physically, and still maintain my current online presence. Sure, my online presence is linked to my real identity, but as long as I don't update that information to say that I moved to Canada, it shouldn't make much of a difference.

reply.shortExcerpt: As someone who does skiptracing, most of what he says is true. But I would hope that the majority of lifehacker readers would never be in a position where they feel that they need to live in the shadows. A lot of the people who try to skip are typically people who owe a lot of money and want to avoid the…
reply.original:
As someone who does skiptracing, most of what he says is true. But I would hope that the majority of lifehacker readers would never be in a position where they feel that they need to live in the shadows. A lot of the people who try to skip are typically people who owe a lot of money and want to avoid the responsibility that comes with borrowing it. Many of these people think they are helping themselves by taking these extreme measures to avoid being found, when in reality, they are severely reducing the quality of their lives and potentially committing crimes.

ProTip: Intentionally providing false information on applications for credit and things of that nature is a crime. It's a pretty common crime amongst deadbeats and isn't often prosecuted...but if found out, it can invalidate someone's ability to legally discharge a debt in bankruptcy.

items.shortExcerpt: @jabber: Except that the book shows you how to do this "legally". Meaning somewhere there is a paper trail. Someone could find you.
items.original:
@jabber: Except that the book shows you how to do this "legally". Meaning somewhere there is a paper trail. Someone could find you.

Honestly, I don't think the government is watching everyone. There's just to many people in the U.S., and government workers are too lazy. I know, my father is one, as well as my husband.
